title: Repeatedly connecting to port on host.docker.internal hangs if the server quits and restarts
body:
### Issue Description

podman version:
```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      5.2.0
API Version:  5.2.0
Go Version:   go1.22.5
Git Commit:   b22d5c61eef93475413724f49fd6a32980d2c746
Built:        Fri Aug  2 05:05:53 2024
OS/Arch:      darwin/arm64

Server:       Podman Engine
Version:      5.1.2
API Version:  5.1.2
Go Version:   go1.22.5
Built:        Tue Jul  9 17:00:00 2024
OS/Arch:      linux/arm64
```

Node.js on host: v20.14.0
Node.js in container: v20.13.1

Host:
Apple M1 Pro
Sonoma 14.6.1

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Install Node.js 20.14.0 on the host.
2. Run
    ```bash
    podman run -it docker.io/library/node:20-alpine3.18 -e '
      process.on("SIGINT", () => process.exit(0));
      (async function loopIt() {
        try {
          console.log(await (await fetch("http://host.docker.internal:9047/info")).text());
        } catch (e) {
          console.log(`An error occurred: ${e.message}`);
        }
        setTimeout(loopIt, 1_000)
      })()
      '
    ```
    on the host to start a HTTP client in a container. It will print out `fetch failed` every second, since there is no server listening.
3. Run
    ```bash
    node -e '
      require("node:http")
        .createServer((req, res) => {
          res.end("Ciao!")
        })
        .listen(9047)
    '
    ```
    on the host to start a HTTP server. The client will start printing "Ciao!" every second.
4. Interrupt the server.



### Describe the results you received

After interrupting the server, the client stops all output.

### Describe the results you expected

After interrupting the server, the client starts printing "fetch failed" again, every second.

### podman info output

### Upstream Latest Release

comments.body: In my original post I wrote that I had podman 5.2.0. I have since tried 5.2.2, and the problem is still there.
please check if you have the same issue as https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/23616

@Luap99 I think so. In my case, I have an HTTP server listening on port 3008 on the host, and I have a process inside a container that accesses it periodically. With 5.2.2, after I interrupt the server on the host, I get, among others

```
gvproxy   63340 gschulhof   46u  IPv4 0x18d07af8485f5ea8      0t0  TCP 127.0.0.1:61727->127.0.0.1:3008 (CLOSED)
```

The next time the process inside the container opens a socket to 3008, it hangs, seemingly indefinitely.

With 5.1.2, there are no lingering sockets in the CLOSED state, so the process inside the container continues to work correctly (in that it reports a failure to connect to the host port).

Thus, I believe this issue is the same as #23616.
Thanks I am going to close this one then as it is a dup
